title: The extinction of the cave bear

passages.title: The extinction of the cave bear
passages.source_html:
The extinction of the cave bear


A


Herve Bocherens, an evolutionary biologist at the University of Tubingen, Germany, says his colleagues find his research methods a little ‘crude’. He dissolves 30,000-year-old animal bones in hydrochloric acid, which is strong enough to burn through metal, soaks the bone solution in lye, cooks it at about 200 degrees Fahrenheit and freeze-dries it, until what’s left is a speck of powder weighing less than one one-hundredth of an ounce. The method may be harsh, but the yield is precious – the biography of a cave bear as told through its chemical components. Bocherens is at the forefront of research on the bear, a European species that died out 25,000 years ago. People have been excavating cave bear remains for hundreds of years – in the Middle Ages, the massive skulls were attributed to dragons – but the past decade has seen a burst of discoveries about how the bears lived and why they became extinct.An abundance of bones has been found from Spain to Romania in caves where the animals once hibernated. Caves are good places to preserve bones, and cave bears had the good sense to die there, Bocherens says.


B


Along with mammoths, lions and woolly rhinos, cave bears (Ursus spelaeus) were once among Europe’s most impressive creatures. Males weighed up to 1,500 pounds,50 per cent more than the largest modern grizzly bears. Cave bears had wider heads than today’s bears, and powerful shoulders and forelimbs. But the relationship between humans and cave bears has been mysterious. Were humans’ prey for the bears, or predators? Were bears the object of worship or fear?


C


Initially cave bears shared the continent of Europe, more than 100,000 years ago, with Neanderthal, a primitive species of humans. Modern humans arrived in Europe about 40,000 years ago, and were soon aware of the bears. They painted images of the animals on cave walls, and carved their likeness in fragments of mammoth tusk. The walls of France’s Chauvet cave are painted with lions, hyenas and bears; dating back 32,000 years these are perhaps the oldest paintings in the world.The painters weren’t the cave’s only occupants: the floor is covered with 150 cave bear skeletons, and its soft clay still holds paw prints. Most dramatically, a cave bear skull was perched on a stone slab in the center of one chamber, placed deliberately by some long-gone cave inhabitant. ‘There’s no way to tell if it was just curiosity that made someone put a skull on the rock, or if it had religious significance,’ says Bocherens.


D


Another discovery, hundreds of miles to the east of Chauvet, would shed light on the relationship between cave bears and humans. The Swabian Jura is a limestone plateau in southwestern Germany that is riddled with caves. A steel gate guards the Hohle Fels cave from vandals and curiosity-seekers. Floodlights in the cave’s main chamber illuminate the ceiling, vaulted like a cathedral above 5,000 square feet of floor space. Long ago, as shown by the bones and tools that archaeologists have found, cave bears and human beings sought shelter here from winter weather.


E


In 2000, University of Tubingen paleobiologist Susanne Munzel unearthed a bear vertebra with a tiny triangular piece of flint embedded in it. The flint was likely a broken spear point, hard evidence of a successful bear hunt 29,000 years ago. Munzel also found bear bones that had clearly been scraped by stone tools. Evidence for bears being a food source for early humans was shown by cut marks on skulls and leg bones where the flesh had been cut away. Many of the bones were from baby bears, perhaps caught while hibernating.


F


Cave bears disappeared not long after humans spread throughout Europe, and one theory is that hunting led to the bears’ extinction. Herve Bocherens’ test tubes may hold the clues.Running his white powder through a mass spectrometer, he identifies different isotopes, or chemical forms, of elements such as carbon and nitrogen that reflect what the bears were eating and how quickly they grew. After studying hundreds of bones from dozens of sites in Europe, Bocherens has found that cave bears ate mainly plants.


G


This would have made bears particularly vulnerable to the last Ice Age, which began around 30,000 years ago. This Ice Age shortened or eliminated growing seasons, and altered the distributions of plant species across Europe. Cave bears began to move from their old territories, according to a DNA analysis of teeth found near the Danube River. The cave bear population there was relatively stable, with the same genetic patterns showing up generation after generation.But about 28,000 years ago, newcomers with different DNA patterns arrived – a possible sign of hungry bears suddenly on the move.


H


But changes in the climate can’t be solely to blame for the bears’ extinction. According to the latest study, by Erik Trinkaus and his colleagues at the Max Planck Institute, cave bear populations began a long, slow decline 50,000 years ago – well before the climate began to change. The new study supports a different explanation for the cave bears’ demise. As Neanderthals, and then a growing population of modern humans moved into the caves of Europe, cave bears had fewer safe places to hibernate. An acute housing shortage may have been the final blow for these magnificent beasts.

groups.title: Questions 1-13
groups.source_html:
Questions 1 – 6


Choose TRUE if the statement agrees with the information given in the text, choose FALSE if the statement contradicts the information, or choose NOT GIVEN if there is no information on this.

1

Bocherens' findings on cave bears involve measuring the length of complete bones.

正确答案： B. FALSE

定位关键信息：Bocherens’ findings, length, bones


原文：A段, “He dissolves 30,000-year-old animal bones in hydrochloric acid, which is strong enough to burn through metal, soaks the bone solution in lye, cooks it at about 200 degrees Fahrenheit and freeze-dries it, until what’s left is a speck of powder weighing less than one one-hundredth of an ounce.The method may be harsh, but the yield is precious – the biography of a cave bear as told through its chemical components.”


解题思路：

本题题目问的是“Bocherens 的研究发现是否受到测量完整骨骼长度的影响”。句中involve一词意思为“受到影响”（If a situation involves someone or something, he, she, or it is affected by it.——Cambridge Dictionary）而非“包括”（If an activity, situation, etc. involves something, that thing is a part of the activity, etc.——Cambridge Dictionary）。题目中findings对应原文the biography of a cave bear，原文提到这一发现是Bocherens 通过化学方法分析骨头的化学成分而不是测量骨头长度得出的。

2

Bocherens was the first person to conduct analytical research on cave bears in a laboratory.

正确答案： C. NOT GIVEN

Q2, Not Given

定位关键词：

the first person，research on cave bears


原文：

A段, “Bocherens is at the forefront of research on the bear, a European species that died out 25,000 years ago. People have been excavating cave bear remains for hundreds of years – in the Middle Ages, the massive skulls were attributed to dragons – but the past decade has seen a burst of discoveries about how the bears lived and why they became extinct.”


解析：

原文提到Bocherens处于相关研究前沿，并不能确定是否为第一个相关研究者。


3

Cave bears have been extinct for 25,000 years.

正确答案： A. TRUE

Q3, TRUE

定位关键词：

extinct 25,000 years ago


原文：

A段, “Bocherens is at the forefront of research on the bear, a European species that died out 25,000 years ago.”


解析：

原文提到穴居熊在25000年前灭绝了。


同义替换：

died out=extinct

4

At one time people thought the excavated remains of bears were those of dragons.

正确答案： A. TRUE

Q4, TRUE

定位关键词：

dragons


原文：

A段, “...in the Middle Ages, the massive skulls were attributed to dragons.”


解析：

原文提到中世纪的人们以为穴居熊头骨是龙的头骨。

5

Modern grizzly bears are similar in size to cave bears.

正确答案： B. FALSE

Q5, FALSE

定位关键词：

Modern grizzly bears


原文：

B段, “Males weighed up to 1,500 pounds, 50 per cent more than the largest modern grizzly bears. Cave bears had wider heads than today’s bears, and powerful shoulders and forelimbs.”


解析：

原文提到雄性cave bear重达1500磅，比现代最大的灰熊还要重50%. 同时，cave bear的头更大，肩部和前肢的力量更强。

6

Neanderthals understood cave bear behaviour better than modern humans did.

正确答案： C. NOT GIVEN

Q6, Not Given

定位关键词：

Neanderthals


原文：

C段, “Initially cave bears shared the continent of Europe, more than 100,000 years ago, with Neanderthal, a primitive species of humans. Modern humans arrived in Europe about 40,000 years ago, and were soon aware of the bears.”


解析：

原文没有提到哪个人种更了解穴居熊的信息。

Questions 7 – 13


Complete the sentences.Write ONE WORD ONLY in each gap.

7


Research into the existence and disappearance of cave bears


Evidence from the Chauvet cave in France

• Paintings on the walls include bears.

• Over a hundred of cave bears together with paw prints were found on the floor of the cave.

• The of a cave bear was found in a prominent position in the cave.


Evidence from the Hohle Fels cave in the Swabian Jura in Germany

• Bones and tools found in the cave indicate it provided for both bears and humans.


Susanne Munzel's findings

• A spear point in the vertebra of a bear indicates that a hunt had taken place.

• Marks on the bones of bears indicate the removal of flesh.


Herve Bocherens' findings

Isotopes from the bones indicate that were the cave bears' preferred food.


Factors leading to the disappearance of the cave bear

• Climate change

– The Ice Age changed vegetation growth in .

– Bears were forced to migrate.

– Support for the above explanation comes from a study of changes in the DNA in the of the population of cave bears near the Danube River at this time.


• Human population expansion

– Competition from Neanderthals and modern humans for caves may have reduced opportunities for cave bears to .

正确答案： 7 skeletons  |  8 skull  |  9 shelter  |  10 plants  |  11 Europe  |  12 teeth  |  13 hibernate
Question 7

定位关键信息：Chauvet cave, a hundred, paw prints

 

原文：C段, “The painters weren’t the cave’s only occupants: the floor is covered with 150 cave bear skeletons, and its soft clay still holds paw prints.”

 

解题思路：

原文提到在洞穴的地板上发现了150具熊的骨架，符合题目中的描述，因此答案是“skeletons”。题目中的“Over a hundred”在原文中对应为“150”。

Question 8

定位关键信息：prominent position

 

原文：C段, “Most dramatically, a cave bear skull was perched on a stone slab in the center of one chamber, placed deliberately by some long-gone cave inhabitant.”

 

解题思路：

原文描述了一个熊的头骨被放在一个房间中心的石板上，位置显眼，因此答案是“skull”。题目中的“a prominent position”在原文中对应为“in the center”。

Question 9

定位关键信息：Hohle Fels cave, Bones and tools

 

原文：D段, “Long ago, as shown by the bones and tools that archaeologists have found, cave bears and human beings sought shelter here from winter weather.”

 

解题思路：

原文提到熊和人类都在洞穴中避寒，表明洞穴提供了“shelter”（庇护所），因此答案是“shelter”

Question 10

定位关键信息：Herve Bocherens, Isotopes, food

 

原文：F段, “…he identifies different isotopes, or chemical forms, of elements such as carbon and nitrogen that reflect what the bears were eating and how quickly they grew. After studying hundreds of bones from dozens of sites in Europe, Bocherens has found that cave bears ate mainly plants.”

 

解题思路：

原文通过同位素分析发现洞熊主要吃植物，因此答案是“plants”。题目中的“preferred food”在原文中对应为“ate mainly”。

Question 11

定位关键信息：Climate change, Ice Age, vegetation

 

原文：G段, “This Ice Age shortened or eliminated growing seasons, and altered the distributions of plant species across Europe.”

 

解题思路：

原文提到冰河时代改变了欧洲的植物分布，因此答案是“Europe”。题目中的“changed vegetation growth”在原文中对应为“altered the distributions of plant species across”。

Question 12

定位关键信息：DNA

 

原文：G段, “Cave bears began to move from their old territories, according to a DNA analysis of teeth found near the Danube River.”

 

解题思路：

原文提到对在多瑙河附近发现的牙齿进行了DNA分析，因此答案是“teeth”。题目中的“DNA in the... population of cave bears”在原文中对应为“DNA analysis of teeth”。

Question 13

定位关键信息：Competition, Neanderthals


原文：H段, “The new study supports a different explanation for the cave bears’ demise. As Neanderthals, and then a growing population of modern humans moved into the caves of Europe, cave bears had fewer safe places to hibernate.” 


解题思路：

原文提到由于人类的竞争，洞熊冬眠的安全地点减少，因此答案是“hibernate”。题目中的“reduce the opportunities for cave bears”在原文中对应为“fewer safe places”。
